coredata.dc:title: Dynamic encryption enabled by circularly polarized carbon dot-based afterglow composites featuring hierarchical tri-mode thermochromic emission
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haozhi
Jin, Longming
Mo, Wanqi
Hong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Dynamic encryption with multiple stimuli responses has sparked significant research interest in the fields of information security and anti-counterfeiting. The development of persistent luminescent materials featuring multi-modal and multi-tiered anti-counterfeiting properties is of substantial importance. In this work, we present a carbon dot-based afterglow composite that exhibits hierarchical thermochromic emission, along with time- and excitation-dependent afterglow, as well as circularly polarized luminescence (CPL). Notably, the composite demonstrates reversible thermochromic fluorescence (FL) emission with almost unchanged afterglow properties. Moreover, the coexistent room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) of the system results in temperature-dependent afterglow, along with excitation- and time-dependent afterglow color caused by the multiple structures of the carbon dots. Furthermore, the precursor of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) induces double chiral characteristics in the system, arising from the right-handed CNCs and the left-handed chiral nematic self-assembly of the CNCs, leading to invertible handedness and tunable dissymmetric factors. With a photoluminescence quantum efficiency of 50.4% and an afterglow efficiency of 38.9%, the system exhibits a five-in-one quintuple responsiveness and demonstrates multiple security features in response to independent stimuli, making it a promising candidate for applications in security features and sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dynamic encryption
Carbon dots
Persistent luminescence
Thermochromic emission
Circular polarized luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic for the preparation process of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite. (b) SEM images of CNCs, chiral nematic CNCs@SiO2, B-CDs@SiO2 composite, along with the TEM image of B-CDs (the insets show the HRTEM image and the size distribution of B-CDs, respectively). (c) Hierarchical thermochromic emission and multi-dimensional stimuli-responsive behavior of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite.

(a) XRD patterns of CNCs, CNCs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2 composite, B-CDs@SiO2 composite, CDs without SiO2 and B-CDs without SiO2; (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs and B-CDs without SiO2. (c) XPS survey spectra of CDs@SiO2 composite and B-CDs@SiO2 composite. (d) High-resolution XPS B1s and C1s spectra deconvoluted fitting results of B-CDs@SiO2 composite.

Normalized (a) steady-state and (b) delayed emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 composite under various excitation wavelength. (c) Trajectory of excitation-dependent phosphorescence in the CIE coordinate diagram. (d) Excitation-dependent phosphorescence mapping and (e) digital images for afterglow of B-CDs@SiO2 composite under different excitation wavelength (the digital images are recorded at intervals of 0.5 s from 0.5 s to 4.0 s).

Normalized time-resolved delayed emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 composite after turning off the UV lamp of (a) 254 nm and (b) 310 nm. Trajectory of time-dependent phosphorescence of B-CD@SiO2 composite in the CIE coordinate diagram after turning off the UV lamp of (c) 254 nm and (d) 310 nm. Plots of DF and RTP intensity against time of B-CDs@SiO2 composite after turning off the UV lamp of (e) 254 nm and (f) 310 nm. (g) Digital images of B-CDs@SiO2 composite at different excitation wavelengths with UV irradiation on and off (the digital images are recorded at intervals of 0.2 s from 0.2 s to 3.0 s).

(a) Steady-state and (b) delayed emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 composite at various temperatures by 395 nm excitation and the inset in (b) is the trajectory of temperature-dependent phosphorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 composite in the CIE coordinate diagram after turning off the UV lamp of 395 nm. (c) Plots of the intensity of delayed emission of B-CDs@SiO2 composite with 395 nm excitation at various temperatures. (d) The linear fit of the delayed emission intensity at 470 nm of B-CDs@SiO2 composite versus the excitation power. (e) Digital images of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite at various temperatures with UV light of 395 nm irradiation on and off.

(a) Digital images for the thermochromic FL color revolution of B-CDs@SiO2 composite triggered at 473 K and 673 K in air by 365 nm excitation (compared with the unchanged afterglow). (b and c) Normalized steady-state and delayed emission spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 composite by 365 nm excitation at 303 K. (d) Schematic of the structural evolution of B-CDs@SiO2 composite by treatment at 473 K and 673 K. (e and f) High-resolution XPS spectra and fitting results of C 1 s, and B 1 s of B-CDs@SiO2 treated at 473 K (top) and 673 K (bottom). (g) Digital images for cycle tests of the hierarchical thermochromic property.

The electronic circular dichroism (ECD) spectra, reflectance spectra (normal incident), steady-state FL spectra and corresponding g lum values of the circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) of the chiral nematic (a) B-CDs@SiO2 composite, (b) CDs@SiO2 composite, (c) B-CDs@SiO2 composite after heat-treatment at 473 K for 6 h and (d) BG-CDs@SiO2 composite at viewing angles of 90°, 45° and 0° by 365 nm excitation (The optical path for the measurements is shown in Fig. S20).

Multiply dynamic encryption by stimuli-response of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite. (a) The composition of patterns ‘butterfly’. (b) The hierarchical thermochromic feature with 365 nm excitation of the pattern. (c to e) Excitation-, time- and temperature-dependent afterglow color of a pattern formed by B-CDs@SiO2 composite and CDs@SiO2 composite. Scale bars: 1 cm.
Photophysical parameters for CDs@SiO2 composite and B-CDs@SiO2 composite.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a) excitation wavelengths; b) steady-state PL maxima; c) phosphorescence maxima determined on delayed emission spectra; d) delayed emission lifetimes; e) photoluminescence quantum yields; f) afterglow quantum yields.

head.ce:title: Dynamic encryption enabled by circularly polarized carbon dot-based afterglow composites featuring hierarchical tri-mode thermochromic emission

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Polymeric Composite and Functional Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, Sun Yat-sen University, Guangzhou 510275, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dynamic encryption with multiple stimuli responses has sparked significant research interest in the fields of information security and anti-counterfeiting. The development of persistent luminescent materials featuring multi-modal and multi-tiered anti-counterfeiting properties is of substantial importance. In this work, we present a carbon dot-based afterglow composite that exhibits hierarchical thermochromic emission, along with time- and excitation-dependent afterglow, as well as circularly polarized luminescence (CPL). Notably, the composite demonstrates reversible thermochromic fluorescence (FL) emission with almost unchanged afterglow properties. Moreover, the coexistent room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) of the system results in temperature-dependent afterglow, along with excitation- and time-dependent afterglow color caused by the multiple structures of the carbon dots. Furthermore, the precursor of cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) induces double chiral characteristics in the system, arising from the right-handed CNCs and the left-handed chiral nematic self-assembly of the CNCs, leading to invertible handedness and tunable dissymmetric factors. With a photoluminescence quantum efficiency of 50.4% and an afterglow efficiency of 38.9%, the system exhibits a five-in-one quintuple responsiveness and demonstrates multiple security features in response to independent stimuli, making it a promising candidate for applications in security features and sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent advancements have focused on developing innovative information-storage materials and sophisticated data-encryption techniques to ensure secure data preservation and transfer while enhancing information security through diverse and dynamic anti-counterfeiting features [1]. Afterglow materials, including room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials and delayed fluorescence (DF) materials, are characterized by long lasting light emitting, providing multi-level optical characteristics and effectively preventing security features from being mimicked [2]. Among them, RTP refers to light emission from the excited triplet state (T1) whose excitons originate in intersystem crossing (ISC) from the excited singlet state (S1) [3]. Meanwhile, DF can be divided into two categories: triplet–triplet annihilation and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) referring to transition from T1 to S1 through reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) [4,5]. At present, great success has been achieved in developing afterglow materials with long-lived excited states and stimuli-responsive functions including excitation- [6,7], time- [7–9], humidity- [10,11], and temperature-dependence [12,13].

Carbon dots (CDs), as promising nanomaterials, have demonstrated significant potential due to their abundant raw materials, simple preparation, good biocompatibility, high stability, and low toxicity [14–18]. Embedding CDs into a rigid matrix enables confinement effects that stabilize triplet excitons and generate long afterglow emission [14,19]. Recently, it has been reported that incorporating CDs into rigid matrices, such as aluminum oxide [20], urea [21], boric acid [22], silica, and polymers [19,23–25], can induce persistent luminescence with time- and temperature-dependent properties. This phenomenon is attributed to the dual-mode emission of delayed fluorescence (DF) and room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP), which exhibit distinct wavelengths, lifetimes, and temperature dependence [26,27]. In addition, thermochemical reactions of CDs, such as carbonization, can lead to thermo-responsive emission behaviors, including the activation of RTP and color evolution of fluorescence (FL) [28,29], which are generally irreversible. Moreover, chiral ligands and supramolecular self-assembly can impart circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) to fluorophores (e.g. CDs), introducing an additional optical dimension beyond emission properties [30–34]. Nevertheless, most of the reported carbon-dot systems exhibit fewer than four stimulus-responsive behaviors (Table S1) [35,36]. To address this limitation, equipping dual-mode persistent luminescent CDs with additional reversible thermochromic functionalities and circularly polarized properties could significantly expand their functional scope for multi-level dynamic encryption.

Herein, we propose a strategy to realize a five-in-one dynamic encryption system utilizing boron-doped CDs@SiO2 (B-CDs@SiO2) composites featuring hierarchical tri-mode thermochromic emission and tunable CPL (Fig. 1 c). The B-CDs@SiO2 composite exhibits a photoluminescence quantum efficiency of up to 50.4 % and an afterglow efficiency of 38.9 %, along with time- and excitation-dependent afterglow colors. Moreover, the B-CDs@SiO2 composite demonstrates unprecedented hierarchical tri-mode reversible thermochromic emission: a temperature-dependent afterglow color based on TADF/RTP dual-mode emission and a thermochromic FL color triggered by heat treatment at 473 K, which can be restored by heat treatment at 673 K. In addition, the system exhibits tunable CPL with reversible circular polarization, arising from the precursor of right-handed cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) and their left-handed chiral nematic structures. With these five responsive features, the B-CDs@SiO2 composite holds great promise for multiple dynamic encryption applications.

To achieve effective afterglow emission of CDs, triplet excitons must be populated through efficient spin–orbit coupling to facilitate intersystem crossing (ISC) [37–39]. The vacant p-orbital of boron atoms attracts π-electrons, forming a p-n-π* conjugated system that reduces the minimum unoccupied orbital energy level of CDs, thereby enhancing the ISC process [40]. To exploit this mechanism, boric acid was used as a dopant to fabricate boron-doped CDs (B-CDs) embedded in a rigid SiO2 matrix via calcination of CNCs@SiO2 (Fig. 1a). The rigid SiO2 matrix in the composite can stabilize triplet excitons,whileboron atoms reduce the energy gap (ΔE st) betweentheS1 and T1 states.The CNC liquid crystals co-assembled with hydrolyzed tetraethoxysilane through evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA), resulting in a chiral nematic CNCs@SiO2 composite [41–43]. After infiltrating boric acid into the CNCs@SiO2 system, calcination in air produced B-CDs within the chiral cavities of the SiO2 matrix, which retained the chiral nematic structure of the CNC liquid crystals. Subsequent removal of silica from the B-CDs@SiO2 composite yielded isolated B-CDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images (Fig. 1b) reveal layered, periodic stacking in CNC films, CNCs@SiO2 films, and B-CDs@SiO2 films, confirming that the SiO2 matrix precisely replicates the chiral nematic structure. After calcination, the characteristic cellulose diffraction peak at 23.3° in the XRD patterns of CNCs and CNCs@SiO2 disappears [44], while an amorphous peak around 21° emerges, attributed to the CDs (Fig. 2 a). Both the B-CDs@SiO2 composite and isolated B-CDs exhibit XRD peaks at approximately 28.1°, indicative of boron oxide formation from excess boric acid [40]. Boron oxide is formedthroughthe thermaldecomposition of boric acidduring heating [45].

As shown in the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of Fig. 1b, B-CDs exhibit uniform and well-dispersed quasi-spherical morphologies with an average diameter of ∼ 2.5 nm. The high-resolution (HR) TEM image (inset of Fig. 1b) reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitized carbon [46–48]. Fig. 2b presents the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of non-boron-doped carbon dots (denoted as CDs), which show characteristic peaks at 1421 cm−1 and 1055 cm−1, attributed to C-C and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively [46,49]. The FT-IR spectra of B-CDs confirm the effective co-doping of boron and oxygen, as evidenced by the characteristic absorption peaks at 1480 cm−1 (B-O) and 1045 cm−1 (B-O-C) [40,49]. Additionally, the high-resolution B1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 192.9 eV, 193.8 eV, and 194.9 eV, corresponding to BCO2, B2O3, and B-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d). These results confirm both the presence of B2O3 and the successful simultaneous co-doping of boron and oxygen into B-CDs [45,50].

The resulting B-CDs@SiO2 composite exhibited superior photophysical properties compared to the control group without boron doping (CDs@SiO2). The B-CDs@SiO2 composite displayed broadband white light FL, in contrast to the blue FL of the CDs@SiO2 composite, with significantly improved photoluminescence quantum yields (ΦFl) and afterglow quantum yields (ΦPh) (Table 1 ). Additionally, boron doping introduced a second afterglow emission region under 395 nm excitation (Fig. S2 and Table 1), with an optimized boric acid concentration of 0.8 M (Fig. S3 and S4). At room temperature (RT), the B-CDs@SiO2 composite emitted a bright yellowish-green afterglow (τ = 395 ms) upon 395 nm excitation, whereas the CDs@SiO2 composite showed a weak and short-lived afterglow (τ = 60 ms). Furthermore, boric acid was found to be a more effective dopant than 1,4-phenylenebisboronic acid (Table S2 and S3, Fig. S5 and S6). Beyond boron doping, the rigid SiO2 matrix played a critical role in stabilizing triplet excitons to enable effective persistent luminescence, as the afterglow of B-CDs lacking the SiO2 matrix could not be observed by the naked eye (Fig. S7).

As shown in Fig. 4a to f, the yellowish-green room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) (Ex = 254 nm, Em = 530 nm, τ = 288 ms; Ex = 310 nm, Em = 535 nm, τ = 232 ms) dominates the delayed emission immediately after irradiation ceases. Over time, the yellowish-green afterglow diminishes, giving way to a blue afterglow emission (Ex = 254 nm, Em = 470 nm, τ = 312 ms; Ex = 310 nm, Em = 470 nm, τ = 290 ms), resulting in time-dependent afterglow color. The excitation-dependent and time-resolved afterglow color can be attributed to multiple triplet-excited states in the B-CDs with multi-dispersed structures [28,51].

Furthermore, the temperature-dependent characteristics of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite are demonstrated by the steady-state and delayed emission spectra in Fig. 5 . Two bands with opposite correlations between intensity and temperature are observed in the steady-state and delayed emission spectra (at 470 nm and 550 nm) under 395 nm excitation (Fig. 5a and b). Since the delayed emission band peaking at 550 nm persists from RT down to 77 K (Fig. 5c), this band can be assigned to RTP emission, as thermally activated processes are restricted at low temperatures, ruling out the possibility of DF. Meanwhile, the delayed emission band at 470 nm gradually intensifies as the temperature increases from RT to 423 K, while the band at 550 nm declines, demonstrating the DF feature of the 470 nm band due to its positive temperature correlation caused by the promoted RISC process at high temperatures [5,52,53]. Regarding the nature of DF, the slope of the excitation power-delayed emission intensity (at 470 nm) curve is fitted to be 1.13 (Fig. 5d), indicating its TADF nature arising from a single-photon absorption process [52,54–57].

The dual-afterglow mechanism involving RTP and TADF can be described as follows:
(1)
Δ
E
st
= 1240 /
λ
Fl
- 1240 /
λ
Ph
where λ Fl and λ Ph represent the fluorescence and phosphorescence wavelengths, respectively [46]. At 77 K, the ΔE st values of the CDs@SiO2 and B-CDs@SiO2 composites were calculated using Equation (1) as 0.36 eV and 0.04 eV, respectively (Fig. S8). The low ΔE st values observed for both composites suggest enhanced intersystem crossing (ISC), favoring the generation of triplet-state excitons and creating favorable conditions for phosphorescence. Notably, the significantly smallerΔE st value of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite indicates that boron-oxygen co-doping further reduces the energy gap, thereby promoting reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) and enabling efficient TADF [40,51,58,59]. This reducedΔE st in B-CDs@SiO2 confirms the coexistence of ISC and RISC processes between the S1 and T1 states, which synergistically drive both TADF and RTP emissions (Fig. S8). As the dominant delayed emission gradually shifts from the RTP emission at 550 nm to the DF intensity at 470 nm with increasing temperature (from 273 K to 473 K), the persistent luminescent color excited by 395 nm varies reversibly between yellowish-green and blue (Fig. 5e), achieving temperature-dependent afterglow color evolution in the system.

Interestingly, a hierarchical thermochromic FL behavior was observed for the B-CDs@SiO2 composite. The FL color of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite shifts reversibly from yellowish-green to white-blue as the temperature increases from 293 K to 473 K under 365 nm excitation (Fig. S9c), similar to the temperature-dependent afterglow color. However, when the B-CDs@SiO2 composite was heated at 473 K for 6 h (As shown in Fig. S10, there is almost no observable change in the FL color at RT when the sample undergoes short-term heat treatment at 473 K for less than 1 h.) and then cooled down to RT, the FL color changed irreversibly to blue, while the afterglow color returned reversibly to yellowish-green (Fig. 6 a). This process can be reversed by heat treatment at 673 K for 4 h, providing an additional type of reversible thermochromic function apart from the TADF/RTP mechanism. As shown in Fig. 6g and Fig. S11, the thermochromic FL exhibits good repeatability through cyclic heat treatment at 473 K and 673 K, resulting in an independent thermochromic performance distinct from the temperature-dependent afterglow. In addition, time-dependent and excitation-dependent afterglow can still be observed when the B-CDs@SiO2 composite is heated at 473 K and 673 K (Fig. S12), indicating that the thermochromic FL has almost no effect on the afterglow properties.

To understand the chemical nature of the thermochromic FL color revolution triggered at 473 K and 673 K, the chemical structures of the B-CDs were analyzed by XPS. It is found that the binding energy value of C-C/C=C shifts to lower values, and the content of BCO2 significantly increases for the B-CDs@SiO2 composite treated at 473 K in the deconvoluted C 1 s and B 1 s results (Fig. 6e and 6f), indicating the formation of additional C-B structures at 473 K [60]. Correspondingly, the treatment at 673 K likely leads to the breakage of C-B bonds. Furthermore, the formation of C-B bonds at 473 K is proven to be a synergistic process with oxidation, as evidenced by the fact that heating in nitrogen at 473 K does not lead to the expected blue FL (Fig. S13a and S13c), which is also supported by the increased C=O signal after 473 K treatment in air (Fig. 6e). It is believed that the thermal oxidation of the B-CDs at 473 K occurred, and boronic ester (BCO2) could form on the surface with boron oxide nearby. Moreover, the reverse process by treatment at 673 K in nitrogen is less effective compared to that in air (Fig. S13b and S13d), as the blue FL color turns to white (a mixture of blue FL at 440 nm and yellowish-green FL at 560 nm) instead of yellowish-green, indicating that oxidation is also necessary for complete graphitization and breakage of C-B bonds (decomposition of boronic ester into boron oxide and CO2) [61]. The reversible breakage and formation of C-B bonds on the surface of B-CDs were further demonstrated by the control groups of CDs@SiO2 composite without boron. CDs@SiO2 do not show the thermochromic FL color revolution triggered at 473 K (Fig. S14), indicating that the boron oxide attached closely to the B-CDs is the key to the thermochromic FL. In addition, the constant afterglow color is primarily generated in the core of the B-CDs [15], while the reversible formation-breakage of C-B bonds on the B-CDs’ surface governs the thermochromic FL color transition triggered at 473 K and 673 K (Fig. 6d). Consequently, the formation of additional C-B structures at 473 K and the breakage of C-B bonds at 673 K on the B-CDs’ surface do not affect the afterglow color of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite.

Moreover, the B-CDs@SiO2 composite exhibits circularly polarized characteristics caused by the left-handed chiral nematic structures of the CNC@SiO2 templates (Fig. S15) [62–65], and the right-handed twisted cellulose fibrils of CNCs [41,66]. The dissymmetry factor (g lum) of B-CDs@SiO2 is calculated for CPL magnitude evaluation, which is defined as:
(2)
g
lum
= 2 ×
I L
-
I R
I L
+
I R
where I L and I R refer to the L-CPL and R-CPL intensity, respectively [67–70]. By comparing the CPL spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 with CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 7 a and b), the g lum for CDs@SiO2 (0.022, viewing angle = 90°) is much higher than that for B-CDs@SiO2 (0.008, viewing angle = 90°), as the PL emission from B-CDs@SiO2 (peaked at 560 nm) is far from the chiral photonic band gap (PBG) of B-CDs@SiO2 (peaked at 313 nm). The chiral nematic structure of the CNCs liquid crystal template leads to the reflectance feature and positive electronic circular dichroism (ECD) signal of the system, namely the chiral PBG. The effect of the PBG is further supported by the enhanced g lum of B-CDs@SiO2 (0.017, viewing angle = 90°) after heat treatment at 473 K (Fig. 7c), as the PL blue-shifts to 440 nm, partially overlapping with the PBG, similar to the case of CDs@SiO2 (PL peaked at 430 nm). When the PBG is tuned by introducing glucose as a spacer into the CNCs@SiO2 system during the EISA process to increase the helical pitch of the chiral nematic structures (Fig. S16a and b) [71], the resulting system (denoted as BG-CDs@SiO2) exhibits a red-shifted peak reflection wavelength to 550 nm, as demonstrated by the ECD spectra (Fig. S16c) and reflectance spectra (Fig. S16d). As the PBG (peaked at 550 nm) significantly overlaps with the PL emission (peaked at 560 nm) for BG-CDs@SiO2, the g lum (viewing angle = 90°) for the FL is boosted to 0.027 (Fig. 7d).

In addition, due to the anisotropy of the chiral nematic structure, the g lum values of chiral nematic composites vary with different viewing angles (which is further confirmed by different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. S17a-c). All g lum values with viewing angles of 0° (viewing parallel to the surface of the sample) exhibit negative values (Fig. 7), as the left-handed nematic structure does not cause a PBG in that direction, where the right-handed twisted cellulose fibrils of CNCs align in each plane of the nematic structure to induce strong right-handed effects. On the contrary, the disordered control groups (preventing CNC self-assembly via electrolytes) for B-CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2, and BG-CDs@SiO2 show positive g lum values without angle dependence (Fig. S18), likely due to the short-range order in isotropic distribution, supporting that the negative g lum values (with viewing angles of 0°) of chiral nematic B-CDs@SiO2, CDs@SiO2, and BG-CDs@SiO2 arise from the aligned CNC templates. More interestingly, the negative g lum (−0.008 at 440 nm) for B-CDs@SiO2 with a viewing angle of 45° (Fig. 7a) turns into a positive g lum (0.014 at 440 nm) after heat treatment at 473 K (Fig. 7c), achieving in-situ circular polarization reversal through the reversible thermochromic PL effects. In summary, two responsive CPL properties are found in the B-CDs@SiO2 system: angle-dependent CPL and thermo-responsive CPL (Fig. S19).

Taking advantage of the multiply stimuli-responsive functions of the composites, encryption patterns are fabricated to demonstrate their potential applications in dynamic encryption (Fig. 8 ). As shown in Fig. 8a, a ‘butterfly’ pattern is formed by B-CDs@SiO2 powder (outer part and circles) and CDs@SiO2 powder (inner part). Under 365 nm excitation at RT, the pattern emits PL composed of yellow for the outer part and blue for the inner part, along with yellowish-green afterglow for the entire pattern (Fig. 8b). When heated to 473 K, the FL and afterglow of the entire pattern turn blue. After being treated at 473 K for 6 h, the FL of the pattern remains blue when cooled down to RT, while the afterglow of the pattern reverts to yellowish-green. Through heat treatment at 673 K, the PL and afterglow of the entire pattern are recovered to the initial state, demonstrating a hierarchical and reversible thermochromic feature.

Moreover, time-, excitation-, and temperature-dependent color evolutions of afterglow can be observed for the pattern, as shown in Fig. 8c. The FL color of the pattern remains almost constant under various UV excitation wavelengths, while the afterglow color varies from blue to yellowish under different excitation wavelengths. Additionally, the outer part and circles of the ‘butterfly’ exhibit conspicuous time-resolved persistent luminescence, transitioning from green to sky-blue and then to blue after 254 nm excitation (Fig. 8d). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 8e, temperature-responsive FL color can be observed in the outer part of the pattern, while the FL of the inner part remains blue. In contrast, the outer part and the inner part show synchronized afterglow color changes with increasing temperatures.

In addition, the I DF / I RTP ratio (IDF and IRTP are the delayed emission intensities at 470 nm and 550 nm, respectively, representing TADF and RTP) follows a linear relationship described by y = 0.0112x − 3.0158 (R2 = 0.9916) within the temperature range of 273 K to 423 K (Fig. S21), which can be used as a quantitative temperature indicator. Furthermore, the stability of the luminescent properties of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite against water was examined. The delayed emission intensity and duration of luminescence remain almost unchanged after soaking in water for 28 days (Fig. S22), demonstrating the stability provided by the rigid SiO2 matrix. The stability and rich optical properties make this system highly valuable for dynamic anti-counterfeiting applications.

In summary, we have demonstrated an approach to fabricate hierarchical thermochromic boron-doped carbon dots@SiO2 (B-CDs@SiO2) composites with tri-mode thermochromic emission, along with time- and excitation-dependent afterglow and tunable CPL properties. Importantly, the FL of the B-CDs@SiO2 composite at RT shifts from yellowish-green to blue after heat treatment at 473 K in air (without significantly altering the afterglow properties), caused by the generation of boronic ester through concerted reactions of boron oxide and thermal oxidation on the surface of B-CDs. This process can be reversed by heat treatment at 673 K, attributed to the decomposition of the boronic ester. In addition, the TADF/RTP dual-mode emission leads to the temperature-dependent afterglow color of the system, and the composite exhibits excitation- and time-dependent afterglow color, providing multiple dynamic encryption features that are independent of the thermochromic FL. Furthermore, the precursor of CNCs induces double chiral characteristics in the B-CDs@SiO2, with right-handed chirality caused by the CNCs and left-handed chirality arising from the chiral nematic structure, resulting in CPL features tuned by the chiral photonic stopband. This study proposes a strategy for achieving persistent luminescent CDs with multiple modes and may expand the scope of multiply responsive CD materials for promising applications in anti-counterfeiting and sensing.

Haozhi Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Longming Jin: Data curation. Wanqi Mo: Data curation. Wei Hong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.162427.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.H. Xiao, P. Weidler, S.S. Lin, C. Wöll, Z.G. Gu, J. Zhang, Chiral metal-organic cluster induced high circularly polarized luminescence of metal-organic framework thin film, Adv. Funct. Mater. 32(34) (2022), 2204289.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Layer‐by‐layer Chiral induction of fluorene‐based metal-organic framework films for circularly polarized luminescence sensing of enantiomers

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Large, crack‐free freestanding Films with chiral nematic structures
